lesson.title: Context
lesson.summary: Carry deadlines, cancellations, and request-scoped values across API boundaries.

sections.heading: What is context
sections.bodyHtml:
The context package lets you pass deadlines, cancellation signals, and request-scoped values between goroutines and across API boundaries.

A context.Context is an immutable tree. You create a parent context and derive child contexts with additional behaviour:

ctx := context.Background()   // root, never cancelled
ctx = context.TODO()           // placeholder when unsure

sections.heading: Cancellation and deadlines
sections.bodyHtml:
Use context.WithCancel or context.WithTimeout to create contexts that can cancel:

ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 2*time.Second)
defer cancel()  // always call cancel to release resources

// Pass ctx to a blocking operation
result, err := someOperation(ctx)
if errors.Is(err, context.DeadlineExceeded) {
    fmt.Println("operation timed out")
}

Operations should check ctx.Done() periodically and return when it fires:

select {
case <-ctx.Done():
    return ctx.Err()
case result := <-ch:
    return result, nil
}

sections.heading: Context values
sections.bodyHtml:
Pass request-scoped data (like request IDs or auth tokens) with context.WithValue. Use custom types for keys to avoid collisions:

type ctxKey string
const keyRequestID ctxKey = "request_id"

ctx = context.WithValue(ctx, keyRequestID, "req-123")

// In a downstream function:
id, ok := ctx.Value(keyRequestID).(string)

Use context values sparingly — explicit parameters are clearer. Good uses: tracing IDs, auth principals, middleware data.

questions.prompt: Where should context.Context appear in a function signature?
How do you create a context that automatically cancels after 3 seconds?
Which channel does a context close when it is cancelled?
